next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 3

Nate called Kassos Development from the kitchen phone Dominic and Sarah kept bolted to the wall beside the refrigerator. Sarah was in the bathroom with the hair dryer running, which felt like cheating. He could hear her cabinet door open and shut, the little clink of bottles, the ordinary apartment noises of a life that had started before him and kept going after the murder. He pressed the receiver harder to his ear and listened to the ring.

Voicemail.

Good. Better than a person.

"Hey, it's Dom." Even now the voice startled him a little, not because it sounded wrong but because it sounded right. It came out of Dominic's throat with no effort at all, warm and rough in the morning, the voice of a man people probably believed the first time. "I'm still not feeling great. I think it was something I ate. I'll try for tomorrow."

He hung up before he could say anything else stupid. He stood there with the receiver in his hand until the dial tone turned mean.

Sarah came out of the bathroom tying her dark hair into a ponytail, one red pen clenched between her teeth while she tugged the elastic tight. She wore a short-sleeved blouse for summer school and carried a stack of photocopied packets against her hip with the resigned care of someone transporting something already doomed to become more work. When she saw him still holding the phone, she took the pen out of her mouth and said, "You call in?"

"Yeah."

"You still look like shit."

"Thanks."

"That wasn't an insult." She crossed to the counter, set the packets down, and checked the stove knobs with her fingerti...
open_hooks_to_carry: The mirror: Nate confronting Dominic's face for the first time
Physical inventory of the apartment — wallet, flip phone, Windows XP desktop, percolator
prev_tail_excerpt:
...y he'd ruined. Wanted, if he was honest, something cinematic enough to justify how long he'd carried this around.

Instead he had done something pettier and more absolute. He had deleted Dominic so completely there had not even been anybody left to hear the accusation.

Nate swallowed against the taste in his mouth. It was not metallic or dramatic. It tasted like sleep and old water and panic warming up.

He needed to move. He needed air. He needed a mirror. He needed ten impossible things in an order he couldn't establish.

But Sarah's hand was still on him, and the instant he shifted her fingers tightened very slightly, the reflex of somebody surfacing just enough to notice a change in the bed.

"Dom," she murmured, not waking, only shaping the name with her lips before sinking again.

The sound went through him like a blade turned gently.

He shut his eyes.

When he opened them, the clock still said 3:47. The date still sat below it in calm red digits, smug in their clarity.

JUL 11 2005.

Twenty years away, in a city where his father was alive and fourteen-year-old Nate Lazar was asleep somewhere under a cheap summer fan, and Nate himself was in Dominic Altieri's bed wearing Dominic Altieri's body while a woman named Sarah slept against him and the only machine that could have returned him existed, if it existed at all, on the far side of two decades.

He looked down one more time at the broad left hand on the sheet, at the scar crossing the knuckle, and understood the shape of what he had done.

He had not gone to Dominic Altieri.

He had taken Dominic Altieri's place.
state_deltas_required: The immediate physical and logistical disorientation of being in a stranger's body in a world twenty years behind the one Nate left
Nate understands the scope of his situation — he is Dominic, permanently — and begins to form a survival strategy built on learning the contours of a life he stole.
